hypothesis: A multi-task learning model that simultaneously predicts patient readmissions and lengths of stay using electronic health records outperforms a traditional separate model approach for a cohort of heart failure patients at a regional hospital.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for heart failure management
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Learning for Heart Failure Readmission and Length-of-Stay Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Cohort Definition

The study uses electronic health records from the regional hospital spanning 36 months (January 2022–December 2024). The cohort comprises all patients with a heart failure diagnosis code (ICD-10 I50.x) recorded at hospital discharge, age ≥18 years, with complete records for all required features. Patients with fewer than 2 prior visits in the lookback window or incomplete outcome labels are excluded. The expected sample size is approximately 5,000 patients, encompassing 6,500–7,000 index hospitalizations (accounting for readmissions). This dataset is sufficiently large to support both multi-task and baseline models and permits robust subgroup analysis.

### 2. Label and Outcome Definition

Readmission is defined as an unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge for any cause (binary outcome). Length of stay (LoS) is defined as the calendar number of days from admission to discharge for the index hospitalization (continuous outcome). Both outcomes are extracted from structured EHR fields and locked at discharge; no post-discharge follow-up data is used, preventing label leakage. Outcome observation is complete for all patients in the cohort by the end of the study window.

### 3. Data Split and Temporal Strategy

The data is stratified using a random split across the entire dataset, ensuring consistent representation of both readmission and LoS outcomes within each fold (Train, Validation, Test). The stratification addresses class imbalance across the folds and is crucial for maintaining the predictive performance since the data initially exhibits a 3:1 ratio in class imbalance during various seasonal intervals. This approach supports model building by having balanced datasets and prevents bias introduced by class imbalance.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are derived from four categories: (1) baseline demographics (age, sex, BMI, smoking status, comorbidities as binary flags), (2) prior history (number of prior HF hospitalizations, prior ER visits, medication adherence proxy), (3) admission vitals and labs (BP, HR, RR, SpO₂, temperature, BNP, troponin, creatinine, eGFR, hemoglobin, electrolytes), and (4) temporal context (day of week, seasonal indicator, time since last discharge). Derived features include ejection fraction category and a decompensation severity composite score. All continuous features are standardized (z-score normalization) using statistics computed from the training set only; this standardizer is then applied to validation and test sets. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing values are imputed using training-set means/medians; missingness >30% triggers patient exclusion. Critically, no features are computed from the outcome variables, and discharge summary text is not included in the initial feature set to avoid label leakage.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

The multi-task model consists of a shared encoder (2–3 dense layers, 128–256 units, ReLU activation) feeding into two task-specific heads: a classification head for readmission (sigmoid, 1 output) and a regression head for LoS (linear, 1 output). The loss function is a weighted combination: L_total = α · L_readmission + (1 − α) · L_LoS, where α is tuned via grid search (α ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}) on the validation set.

The baseline comprises two independently trained models: logistic regression for readmission and linear regression for LoS, each using the identical feature set and preprocessing. Both are regularized (L2, tuned over λ ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0} on validation data). A naive baseline predicting empirical mean readmission rate and LoS serves as the floor. The separate-model baseline is fair because it receives equivalent feature engineering, hyperparameter tuning effort, and evaluation conditions.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the validation set. Early stopping is applied if validation loss stagnates for 20 consecutive epochs. The model checkpoint achieving the highest composite metric on validation data is locked and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is never accessed during hyperparameter search.

### 7. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is a composite: (Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4). This weighting reflects the clinical priority: preventing readmissions (primary goal) while optimizing resource use (secondary goal). AUC-ROC captures ranking ability across operating points; RMSE captures absolute prediction error for resource planning. Secondary metrics include readmission precision/recall/F1, LoS MAE, per-subgroup performance (age, sex, severity quartile, race/ethnicity), and prediction calibration (expected calibration error for readmission probabilities). Fairness analysis stratifies results by patient demographics and care pathway (unit, physician) to detect confounding by institutional practice.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

Physician/protocol heterogeneity is addressed through stratification by hospital unit and admitting team during train/val/test splits. Post-hoc subgroup analysis isolates patient-level effects from provider effects. Patient heterogeneity is examined via stratified performance reporting. Temporal drift is assessed by comparing cohort characteristics and outcome baselines between train and test. All exclusion criteria and preprocessing decisions are pre-specified; no post-hoc data removal occurs based on results.

### 9. Scope of Inference

This design establishes whether multi-task learning outperforms separate models for heart failure readmission and LoS prediction **at this regional hospital, for this patient population, within this time window**. The stratified split does not establish causality or clinical efficacy of the predictions—only comparative model performance. Prospective clinical validation and provider workflow integration studies are separate, necessary steps before deployment.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a stratified random split on temporally ordered patient data, which violates the chronological independence required for valid model evaluation. This risks including future patient records in the training set while testing on earlier ones, allowing the model to 'see the future' and producing optimistically biased performance estimates. In time-series clinical data, temporal leakage undermines the validity of generalization claims and invalidates the operational relevance of the results.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified random split fails to control for class imbalance and leads to unstable performance estimates.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification on outcome labels in a random split does control class imbalance effectively; that aspect of the design is methodologically sound. The flaw is not the stratification itself, but the use of random splitting instead of temporal splitting in a time-dependent dataset. A reviewer who objects to stratification on grounds of imbalance is misapplying ML principles: here, balanced folds are beneficial, but insufficient without proper temporal safeguards.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The correct outcome requires the critic to identify that the random split, despite stratifying outcomes, introduces temporal leakage by breaking chronological order, which invalidates the evaluation of real-world generalization. The final verdict must conclude that the model performance cannot be trusted due to future data contamination in training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly links time-series structure in EHR data to the need for temporal splits and explains that random splitting, even when stratified, allows future data to influence training, thus inflating performance artificially.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Learning for Heart Failure Readmission and Length-of-Stay Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Cohort Definition

The study uses electronic health records from the regional hospital spanning 36 months (January 2022–December 2024). The cohort comprises all patients with a heart failure diagnosis code (ICD-10 I50.x) recorded at hospital discharge, age ≥18 years, with complete records for all required features. Patients with fewer than 2 prior visits in the lookback window or incomplete outcome labels are excluded. The expected sample size is approximately 5,000 patients, encompassing 6,500–7,000 index hospitalizations (accounting for readmissions). This dataset is sufficiently large to support both multi-task and baseline models and permits robust subgroup analysis.

### 2. Label and Outcome Definition

Readmission is defined as an unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge for any cause (binary outcome). Length of stay (LoS) is defined as the calendar number of days from admission to discharge for the index hospitalization (continuous outcome). Both outcomes are extracted from structured EHR fields and locked at discharge; no post-discharge follow-up data is used, preventing label leakage. Outcome observation is complete for all patients in the cohort by the end of the study window.

### 3. Data Split and Temporal Strategy

The data is split temporally by discharge date: training (Jan 2022–Aug 2023, n ≈ 3,500), validation (Sep 2023–Nov 2023, n ≈ 800), and test (Dec 2023–Dec 2024, n ≈ 1,700). Within each fold, records are stratified by admission severity (NYHA functional class or ejection fraction quartile) to ensure balanced representation of outcome distributions. This temporal split is essential because it mirrors real-world deployment—the model is trained on historical data and prospectively evaluated on future, unseen patients. It eliminates future information leakage and tests generalization over time. Stratification ensures that both readmission rates and LoS distributions are representative in each fold, mitigating outcome imbalance and enabling fair comparison between model architectures.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are derived from four categories: (1) baseline demographics (age, sex, BMI, smoking status, comorbidities as binary flags), (2) prior history (number of prior HF hospitalizations, prior ER visits, medication adherence proxy), (3) admission vitals and labs (BP, HR, RR, SpO₂, temperature, BNP, troponin, creatinine, eGFR, hemoglobin, electrolytes), and (4) temporal context (day of week, seasonal indicator, time since last discharge). Derived features include ejection fraction category and a decompensation severity composite score. All continuous features are standardized (z-score normalization) using statistics computed from the training set only; this standardizer is then applied to validation and test sets. Categorical features are one-hot encoded. Missing values are imputed using training-set means/medians; missingness >30% triggers patient exclusion. Critically, no features are computed from the outcome variables, and discharge summary text is not included in the initial feature set to avoid label leakage.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

The multi-task model consists of a shared encoder (2–3 dense layers, 128–256 units, ReLU activation) feeding into two task-specific heads: a classification head for readmission (sigmoid, 1 output) and a regression head for LoS (linear, 1 output). The loss function is a weighted combination: L_total = α · L_readmission + (1 − α) · L_LoS, where α is tuned via grid search (α ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}) on the validation set.

The baseline comprises two independently trained models: logistic regression for readmission and linear regression for LoS, each using the identical feature set and preprocessing. Both are regularized (L2, tuned over λ ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0} on validation data). A naive baseline predicting empirical mean readmission rate and LoS serves as the floor. The separate-model baseline is fair because it receives equivalent feature engineering, hyperparameter tuning effort, and evaluation conditions.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs exclusively on the validation set. Early stopping is applied if validation loss stagnates for 20 consecutive epochs. The model checkpoint achieving the highest composite metric on validation data is locked and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is never accessed during hyperparameter search.

### 7. Primary and Secondary Metrics

The primary metric is a composite: (Readmission AUC-ROC × 0.6) + (LoS RMSE rank × 0.4). This weighting reflects the clinical priority: preventing readmissions (primary goal) while optimizing resource use (secondary goal). AUC-ROC captures ranking ability across operating points; RMSE captures absolute prediction error for resource planning. Secondary metrics include readmission precision/recall/F1, LoS MAE, per-subgroup performance (age, sex, severity quartile, race/ethnicity), and prediction calibration (expected calibration error for readmission probabilities). Fairness analysis stratifies results by patient demographics and care pathway (unit, physician) to detect confounding by institutional practice.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

Physician/protocol heterogeneity is addressed through stratification by hospital unit and admitting team during train/val/test splits. Post-hoc subgroup analysis isolates patient-level effects from provider effects. Patient heterogeneity is examined via stratified performance reporting. Temporal drift is assessed by comparing cohort characteristics and outcome baselines between train and test. All exclusion criteria and preprocessing decisions are pre-specified; no post-hoc data removal occurs based on results.

### 9. Scope of Inference

This design establishes whether multi-task learning outperforms separate models for heart failure readmission and LoS prediction **at this regional hospital, for this patient population, within this time window**. The temporal split does not establish causality or clinical efficacy of the predictions—only comparative model performance. Prospective clinical validation and provider workflow integration studies are separate, necessary steps before deployment.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because while the corrupted design correctly addresses class imbalance through stratification, it violates a domain-specific best practice in temporal clinical data. Evaluators must recognize that stratified random splitting is not sufficient when time matters—domain knowledge is required to detect the risk of temporal leakage, which may not be obvious to those applying generic ML validation heuristics.
